CHAPTER 7 — PROMPTS FOR RESEARCH AND LITERATURE WORK

After this chapter you will be able to:

Chapter 7 — Prompts for Research and Lit — Use Claude to expand and refine search terms for database queries Summarize sources you have already verified and provid
NARRATION

Use Claude to expand and refine search terms for database queries Summarize sources you have already verified and provided Build a literature matrix from provided sources Draft a gap analysis from materials you have assembled Write prompts that prevent Claude from inventing sources or claims Apply a verification checklist before using any Claude-generated research output

// SLIDE 02 — THE CORE DISTINCTION: LEADS VS

THE CORE DISTINCTION: LEADS VS. EVIDENCE

Research work requires you to hold two categories separate throughout every interaction with Claude.

The Core Distinction: Leads vs. Evidence — A lead is a direction worth investigating. A lead might be a research theme, a search term, a potential framework, or a
NARRATION

Research work requires you to hold two categories separate throughout every interaction with Claude. A lead is a direction worth investigating. A lead might be a research theme, a search term, a potential framework, or a suggested author whose work sounds relevant. Leads are cheap and useful. A wrong lead costs you time in a database search; it does not corrupt your argument.

// SLIDE 03 — BEFORE / AFTER: A RESEARCH PRO

BEFORE / AFTER: A RESEARCH PROMPT WALKTHROUGH

You are beginning a literature review on organizational change fatigue in healthcare settings. You need search terms and

Before / After: A Research Prompt Walkth —
NARRATION

You are beginning a literature review on organizational change fatigue in healthcare settings. You need search terms and a sense of the relevant frameworks.

// SLIDE 04 — WORKING WITH SOURCES YOU HAVE

WORKING WITH SOURCES YOU HAVE PROVIDED.

Main research question→Sample or data source→Key finding→Limitation the authors acknowledge
NARRATION

Once you have gathered real sources , PDFs, excerpts, abstracts you have verified , Claude becomes a powerful summarizer, extractor, and synthesizer. The key constraint: you must provide the text. Claude works from what is in the conversation, not from its training memory of what a source might say. Here is the abstract and introduction of a paper I have verified and will cite:

// SLIDE 05 — THE LITERATURE MATRIX

THE LITERATURE MATRIX.

A literature matrix organizes sources across consistent dimensions so you can see patterns, gaps, and conflicts at a gla

The Literature Matrix — I have attached/pasted excerpts from papers on .
NARRATION

A literature matrix organizes sources across consistent dimensions so you can see patterns, gaps, and conflicts at a glance. It is one of the most useful formats Claude can help you build , provided the sources are yours. I have attached/pasted excerpts from papers on .

// SLIDE 06 — THE SOURCE VERIFICATION CHECKL

THE SOURCE VERIFICATION CHECKLIST.

Does the source exist? — Search the journal, database, or DOI directly
Does the source say what Claude claimed? — Read the abstract or full text
Is the citation format correct? — Check author names, year, volume, pages
Did I provide this source, or did Claude generate it? — If generated, treat as lead only
NARRATION

Before you incorporate any Claude-generated research output into your work, apply this checklist. This is the human gate: the review step that keeps a lead from becoming an unverified claim in your writing. Claude's citation hallucination rate varies by model version, domain specificity, and how the prompt was framed. Research as of early 2024 found hallucinated references in a substantial fraction of LLM-generated citations (Walters and Wilder 2023, Alkaissi and McFarlane 2023). The rate is not zero in any current model. Treat verification as mandatory, not optional.

// SLIDE 07 — COPE AND PUBLISHER GUIDANCE

COPE AND PUBLISHER GUIDANCE

The Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE) and many academic publishers now have explicit policies on the use of AI in r

COPE and Publisher Guidance — This means: if a Claude-assisted literature summary contains a fabricated citation that makes it into a submitted paper,
NARRATION

The Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE) and many academic publishers now have explicit policies on the use of AI in research and writing. The core consensus across these policies is that AI tools cannot be listed as authors (because authorship requires accountability for the work), and that researchers are responsible for the accuracy of every claim in their submissions, including those assisted by AI tools (COPE 2023).

// SLIDE 08 — SEARCH HANDOFF PROMPTS

SEARCH HANDOFF PROMPTS.

One of Claude's most underused capabilities in research work is generating search queries formatted for specific databas

Search Handoff Prompts — PubMed/database query prompt:
NARRATION

One of Claude's most underused capabilities in research work is generating search queries formatted for specific databases. This is a lead-generation task with low hallucination risk because you are asking for query strings, not source claims. I am searching PubMed for studies on . My current search string is:

// SLIDE 09 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Asking for a literature review without providing sources.→Treating named frameworks as citations.→Forgetting "not stated in excerpt."→Using the same verification standard for summaries of provided text vs. generated citations.→Accepting a plausible DOI.
NARRATION

Asking for a literature review without providing sources. The output will look like a literature review. It is not a literature review. It is Claude's statistical projection of what a literature review on that topic might say. Treating named frameworks as citations. When Claude names a theoretical framework (e.g., "Lewin's change model"), that is probably a real framework. But the specific source, page, year, and claim about it still require verification. A real framework name does not validate Claude's claims about what it says.

// SLIDE 10 — BRIDGE

BRIDGE

Research produces the raw material; writing turns it into an argument. Chapter 8 takes the same specification discipline

NARRATION

Research produces the raw material; writing turns it into an argument. Chapter 8 takes the same specification discipline into the writing and editing process, where the risks are different: not fabricated citations, but voice drift, fact-invention in prose, and the slow blurring of what you actually claimed versus what Claude generated on your behalf.

// SLIDE 11 — THESIS

THE CORE DISTINCTION: LEADS VS. EVIDENCE

Research work requires you to hold two categories separate throughout every interaction with Claude.

A lead is a direction worth investigating. A lead might be a research theme, a search term, a potential framework, or a

NARRATION

Research work requires you to hold two categories separate throughout every interaction with Claude. A lead is a direction worth investigating. A lead might be a research theme, a search term, a potential framework, or a suggested author whose work sounds relevant. Leads are cheap and useful. A wrong lead costs you time in a database search; it does not corrupt your argument.
